text:
Minimally Invasive Surgery (MIS) offers numerous benefits
1
v over conventional open surgeries,  including faster recovery,
8 smaller incisions, and reduced complications [1], [2]. These
1 advantages translate into significant improvements in patient
4
outcomes,  such as shorter hospital  stays, quicker  return to
5
daily activities, and lower infection risks. However, adoption
0
.
remains limited due to the steep learning curve and extended
1
operating times of manual MIS instruments, prompting the
0
5 rise of  robot-assisted MIS systems  that  enhance dexterity,
2 maneuverability, and precision.
:
MIS instruments are typically classified into two categories
v
i based on their instrument tip designs: (1) wristed instruments,
X and (2) continuum wrist instruments, referred to in this paper
r
as continuum instruments. Continuum instruments provide sig-
a
nificant advantages,  including safer interaction with anatom-
ical structures, and have been applied to various procedures,
such as trans-oral surgeries, single-port access,  transurethral
bladder cancer  interventions, and neurosurgery [3–6]. Most
continuum instruments rely on cable-driven actuation for artic-
ulation, offering significant potential for advanced control pur-
poses through tension feedback measurement, which provides
real-time insights into cable forces. This information enables
precise control of the instrument’s movement and compliance,
allowing for safer and more accurate interaction with delicate
anatomical structures.

Fig. 1: Proposed actuation unit driving a cable-driven contin-
uum instrument.
Early continuum surgical  instruments were actuated man-
ually [2]. In contrast, modern systems incorporate motorized
actuation, significantly enhancing control precision and dex-
terity of the instrument tip. The actuation unit designs such as
those in [5], [7], [8] achieve multi-degrees-of-freedom (DoF)
control but remain bulky, often exceeding the payload limits of
collaborative robotic arms. Although some recent efforts have
reduced the size and weight of actuation units to fit off-the-
shelf collaborative robotic arms [9], there is a lack of tension
feedback available, limiting advanced control strategies.
Accurate external force sensing is critical for safe interac-
tion with delicate tissues [10]. Existing approaches generally
fall into two categories: direct force sensing and shape-based
force estimation. Direct force sensing uses load cells or sensors
in the instrument tip [11], but this can increase size and com-
plexity. Shape-based force estimation infers external  forces
by measuring shape via  intrinsic feedback from actuation
sensors or extrinsic feedback from external systems [7], [12].
Constant-curvature models simplify calculations but can be
inaccurate for multi-segment bends. More advanced models,
like Cosserat rods, offer higher accuracy but are too compu-
tationally heavy for real-time use, which is crucial in surgery
for safe, precise tissue interaction.
Despite these advancements,  two primary challenges re-
main. First, developing compact actuation systems with real-
time tension feedback remains difficult. Existing push-pull or
cable-driven systems [5],  [8]  that  integrate load cells  tend
to be  too large and heavy for  low-payload robotic arms,
limiting practical application. Smaller units typically estimate
tension from motor current, which is prone to noise, reducing
precision and limiting advanced control. Second, achieving
accurate external  force estimation that combines shape and

This  section describes  the proposed actuation unit  for
cable-driven surgical instruments (Fig. 2), focusing on design
requirements, mechanical  layout,  electronics,  and software
integration. The primary goal  is  to achieve a  lightweight,
modular,  and compact  system,  reducing strain on robotic
arms and enhancing ease of handling during delicate surgical

the cable-driving interface, (d) Exploded view of the interface.
procedures. It is designed to deliver real-time tension feedback,
supporting an advanced shape-force sensing framework.
A. Design Requirements
The actuation unit aims to meet several key design require-
ments essential for cable-driven MIS instruments. Compact-
ness is a priority, as the system must reduce size and weight to
ensure seamless mounting on commercial collaborative robotic
arms with limited payload capacities. The design also em-
phasizes compatibility, enabling the actuation unit to support
instruments with two to four degrees of freedom (DoFs), thus
accommodating a range of surgical applications. Modularity
(Detachable)  is another critical  feature,  allowing for quick
instrument release using a pulley-based interface that connects
the instrument cables to the drive motors. This feature is also
important  for ensuring that  the  instrument  can be quickly
detached and cleaned, or safely discarded after use, which
is essential  for maintaining sterile conditions and meeting
clinical hygiene standards in future deployments. To further
enhance its capabilities, the actuation unit includes Actuation
Redundancy, which refers  to using more actuation cables
than the instrument’s degrees of freedom. This feature allows
advanced algorithms to redistribute cable forces, such as mod-
ulating stiffness for optimized performance during complex
procedures. The unit  incorporates Tension-sensing Feedback
to provide precise control through real-time measurement of
cable tension,  improving the accuracy and responsiveness of
the surgical  instruments. Real-time control  is a vital aspect
of the design, ensuring that there is minimal latency between
user commands and instrument motion, which is crucial for
maintaining precision in dynamic surgical environments.
B. Mechanical Design
As  shown  in Fig.  2,  a  four-degree-of-freedom (4-DoF)
actuation unit was developed, each DoF powered by a DC
motor with integrated torque sensing. Four identical cylindrical
modules are arranged on a shared base plate, each module
housing a motor, torque cell, and 3D-printed shell. This design

metadata.section_title: Virtural-work Based Kinematic-Static Modeling of the Continuum Instrument

text:
In this section we present a novel virtual-work based mod-
eling framework to support the force-shape sensing function.
We define the polynomial curvature-based shape state space
and establish the mappings between joint space, shape state
space, and task space. Building on these mappings, at the end,
we lead to a corresponding force sensing method based on
a static model using the virtual-work principle. This method
is represented by polynomial curvature kinematics, offering
a structured approach to model  the continuum instrument’s
deformation.
A. Polynomial Curvature-based Shape Space
Unlike the constant curvature assumption, a more precise
kinematics-based representation of  the shape is polynomial
curvature kinematics [13–15]. Polynomial curvature kinemat-
ics represent the curvature distribution along the arclength of

To validate the proposed system, we conducted a series of
experiments aimed at addressing the challenge of accurately
estimating both shape and force in continuum instruments.
These experiments were designed to evaluate the actuation
unit and shape-force sensing framework. First, vibration tests
analyzed the system characteristics of a single actuation motor
module. Second, tension-sensing feedback was evaluated using
a continuum instrument to ensure accurate force readings dur-
ing articulation. Third, shape-sensing accuracy was validated

through vision-based tip pose measurements. Finally, a load
cell measured contact forces at the instrument tip to validate
external force-sensing capabilities.
A. Single Module Performance Evaluation
An external  torque supply device was designed for  the
vibration test of a single motor module  to simulate real-
world reaction forces and ensure accurate measurement of
the motor’s response under dynamic loading conditions. The
device includes a cover base, ball bearing,  two compression
springs,  connecting flange,  and motor mounting cap. The
springs, partially embedded in grooves on the cover base,
are compressed by the extrusion on the connecting flange as
the motor  rotates, generating reaction torque on the motor

6
Fig. 7: Tension-sensing feedback capability test result:(a) pull
cable 1, release cable 3; (b) pull cable 2, release cable 4; (c)
pull cable 3, release cable 1; (d) pull cable 4, release cable 1.
shaft. Figure 5 shows the vibration test setup,  including an
exploded CAD model, hardware setup, and sectional view.
To characterize  the motor module,  a position chirp signal
was applied to evaluate position tracking and torque reading
performance.
Figure 6  illustrates  the  results,  highlighting  the motor
module’s precise position tracking and its ability to provide
accurate torque feedback under dynamic loading conditions.
The position output closely follows the input with minimal
phase lag,  indicating excellent tracking performance. Torque
measurements from the torque cell were smoother and more
reliable compared to torque derived from motor current, which
exhibited significant noise and sharp spikes during direction
changes, failing to reflect the true external torque load accu-
rately.
B. Continuum Instrument Articulation via All Four Modules
This  subsection contrasts with  the  single-module  tests
by presenting the coordinated operation of all  four motor
modules, demonstrating synchronized tension control across
multiple cables to achieve precise articulation for real-world
applications.
Cable tension is related to the torque cell reading and the
motor pulley radius. Cable tension readings were calibrated
using weights before assembling the motor modules into the
actuation unit, and the accuracy of calibration was verified
by comparing measured tensions against known weight values
with minimal deviation.

MANUSCRIPT SUBMITTED TO 2025 TMECH/AIM FOCUSED SECTION

Fig. 8: Experimental setup for validation of  the shape and
force sensing.
To evaluate the actuation unit’s  tension feedback, exper-
iments were conducted using a custom-built continuum in-
strument consisting of 3D-printed disks, a super-elastic NiTi
backbone, and four pulling cables. Each cable was connected
to a motor pulley mounted on one of  the four actuation
modules. Figure 7 shows  the  results of  tension feedback
tests: different pairs of cables were sequentially activated in
pulling and releasing cycles  to assess performance across
varied control scenarios. The smooth tension readings validate
the system’s capacity for precise and stable control during
dynamic articulation and were reliable across all cases. The
linear  increasing results in all cases also verified the linear
expression derived in (29).
C.

Continuum Instrument Shape Sensing Validation
This subsection presents  the validation of  the proposed
shape sensing framework by comparing computed shapes with
vision-based ground truth measurements under various sce-
narios. Figure 8 depicts the experimental setup for validating
the shape-force sensing method, highlighting key components
such as the USB camera for shape capture and the load cell
for measuring tip contact forces. The setup ensures accurate
assessment of shape and force estimations by capturing the
instrument’s articulation and corresponding tip contact forces
under various scenarios.
Shape data from the camera serve as ground truth, with
Gaussian noise added to the tip pose to replicate real-world
variability in measurements. This simulates environmental and
sensor noise, making the shape-sensing computations more
robust and reflective of practical scenarios. Two articulation
scenarios under free external loading conditions are evaluated:
◦  ◦  ◦  ◦
(1) θ = 45 , δ = 135 ; (2) θ = 67 , δ = −45 .
Figures 9 and 10 show the shape-sensing results, high-
lighting key differences between the constant curvature and
polynomial curvature models. The polynomial curvature model
shows better alignment with ground truth measurements, par-
ticularly in capturing non-uniform curvature changes along
the instrument’s length. Compared to the constant curvature
model,  it  demonstrates higher  accuracy  in both scenarios
tested,  likely due to its capacity to represent more complex

curvature variations. This improved accuracy has important
implications for tasks requiring precise shape estimation.
D. Continuum Instrument Shape-Force Sensing Validation
This subsection highlights the integration of shape sensing
and cable tension feedback to improve external  force esti-
mation accuracy for  the continuum instrument. To validate
the force-sensing capability,  the continuum instrument was
articulated to make contact with a rubber platform, which
was attached to a load cell (Futek Inc.), under varying cable
tensions.
The contact  force estimation requires  the shape state S.
Modal coefficients m0,m1, and m2 were solved using the tip
pose measurements, and the shape reconstruction results are
shown in Fig. 11. Consistent with the shape-sensing validation
under  free-loading conditions, our model outperformed the.
